content.generation_notice: Fresh live v2 baseline arm. Generator=anthropic; judge=openai. The psychology treatment is a PR #29 sensitivity test, not registry admission.
content.public_question: Should Hungary keep its 6- and 8-year gimnázium tracks?
content.summary:
Hungary's decision on its 6-/8-year gimnázium tracks spans a real option space from full preservation to structural abolition, with several intermediate levers available and combinable. Six transformation paths anchor the space. TP-t1 (status quo with monitoring) changes nothing structurally, relying on existing PISA/ABC reporting infrastructure; its leading constraint is that this same infrastructure has a documented history of not translating into policy correction, so the mechanism produces data without adaptation. TP-t2 (delay selection to age 14-16) targets the earliest and most consequential sorting point directly, drawing on Poland's 1999 comprehensive-tier reform, which is associated with PISA and labour-market gains; its binding constraints are workforce capacity (an aging, undersized teaching corps) and durability, since Poland's reform was substantially reversed after 2016 despite measured success, and even non-abolition proposals in Hungary have triggered high-intensity 'communist unification' framing. TP-t3 (preserve tracks, reform admissions) works within the existing Klebelsberg Centre-administered exam process, leveraging the 2000s precedent that administrative desegregation rules produced temporary segregation reductions; its constraint is coverage — church-maintained and private schools sit outside this authority and can absorb displaced selective demand, and that historical precedent also stagnated over time. TP-t4 (zoning/choice/exemption reform without touching track age) isolates a distinct causal channel — geographic sorting and the 2015 Curia exemption permitting segregated maintainer-run schools — leaving admissions and track age untouched; its constraint is that centralization under KLIK has curtailed the very municipal planning autonomy this lever depends on, and it invites direct legal confrontation with maintainer autonomy. TP-t5 (quotas and outreach within existing tracks) targets compositional access without altering the mechanism or timing of selection; its constraint is a genuine empirical uncertainty distinct from any value question — evidence on whether track value-added exists independent of family background is weak, and at least one study found no measurable tertiary benefit, meaning the 'prize' being redistributed may not durably benefit recipients. TP-t6 (comprehensive pilot with opt-in enrichment) explicitly borrows Finland's 1972-77 mechanism while building in awareness of its confounds and of the post-1990s lesson that choice-driven resegregation can reassert itself absent zoning guardrails, linking itself to T4; its constraint is that even a successful pilot may not secure durable political buy-in, and volunteer regions may be unrepresentative.

Across these paths, empirical uncertainty and value choice are distinguishable but entangled. Empirically open questions include: the independent causal effect of track age itself on segregation net of choice and exemptions; where displaced sorting demand would reallocate if early tracking were removed; whether value-added gains from selective tracks persist into labor-market outcomes or reflect background composition; and whether Poland's gains stemmed from tracking delay itself versus concurrent curriculum and workforce investment. Value choices are separate: how much weight to give durability versus ambition (bold structural change risks reversal; incremental change risks entrenching sorting indefinitely); how far central authority should reach into maintainer and religious-institution autonomy that was foundational to the 1989-90 settlement; and whether equal access to prestige institutions has intrinsic worth independent of measured long-run benefit.

Material disciplinary mechanisms recur across lenses: centralized entrance-exam admissions and their SES-linked sorting advantage; maintainer exemptions placing church/private schools outside ministerial authority; the 2015 Curia ruling protecting exemption-based segregation; demographic contraction concentrating cohorts in urban selective tracks; and the historical non-translation of monitoring data into corrective policy. Political mobilization capacity — demonstrated by reframing even mild consultation proposals as ideological unification — constrains any path touching track age or admissions directly, while fiscal exposure is low across all options since none requires large per-pupil funding shifts.

Decision-makers must weigh: whether to accept near-term political safety at the cost of entrenching documented harm; whether to pursue single-lever tractability or multi-channel bundled reform at higher implementation risk; how much central authority to assert over maintainer autonomy; and whether to gather further pilot evidence before acting nationally, given that harms are already well-documented and actively intensifying under demographic contraction. The research most likely to shift this choice concerns displaced-demand reallocation pathways, the durability determinants behind Poland's reversal, the exempt sector's share of measured segregation, and whether any structural design can achieve lasting cross-coalition support in Hungary's veto-heavy political system.
content.title: Early academic selection and the 6/8-year gimnázium
